data.title: bn-3si2: Protocol review selection bug — unrelated merged review satisfies the review gate
data.description:
risk:high — failure-mode checklist required. This IS the review gate.

BUG: 'seal reviews list' is repo-global (43 reviews in this repo), not workspace- or bone-scoped. All five protocol call sites picked from that raw listing without scoping to the bone: review.rs took reviews[0]; resume.rs took the first entry; status.rs looped over every review per held bone; finish.rs and merge.rs each had a duplicate find_review_for_workspace that excluded only 'merged', not other bones.

IMPACT: 'edict protocol review <bone>' selected merged review cr-316fr8 (belonging to bd-3qwo), evaluated the gate against ITS LGTM votes, and returned Ready — letting unreviewed work proceed to finish/merge. Reproduced live on this very bone before the fix: old binary reported 'Status: Ready / Review: cr-2qex (merged) / approved by botbox-security' for bn-3si2, which had no review at all. New binary reports 'Needs Review' and emits the create steps.

FIX: new review_select module. Reviews associate with a bone only via the '<bone-id>: <title>' convention seal_create_cmd emits, so match on that prefix (colon-delimited so bn-24r != bn-24r9) and exclude terminal statuses (merged/abandoned/closed). All five call sites now route through ProtocolContext::{reviews_for_bone, find_review_for_bone}; both duplicated helpers deleted.

FAILURE MODES CONSIDERED:
- Fails CLOSED everywhere: no bone-scoped review found => NeedsReview (gate blocks), never Ready.
- Duplicate live reviews for one bone => the UNAPPROVED one is selected, so the gate stays shut until all are signed off.
- Explicit --review-id naming a merged/abandoned review => Blocked (previously trusted). Title not naming the bone => loud diagnostic (kept non-blocking so hand-titled reviews still work).
- merge.rs could not always resolve a bone ID; added workspace-name fallback (workspaces are 'maw ws create <bone-id>'). If the bone is still unresolvable, no review matches and merge blocks as NeedsReview.
- Known tradeoff: a review whose title does NOT follow '<bone-id>:' is not auto-discovered, so a duplicate review may be created. That is a safe (fail-closed) failure mode; the alternative — matching loosely — is the bypass we are fixing.

TESTS: 10 new unit tests in review_select (prefix collision, multibyte titles, merged-same-bone, merged-other-bone, duplicate preference, case/whitespace). Full suite: 407 passed, 0 failed. No new clippy errors vs trunk baseline (trunk already has 29 pre-existing).

data.body:
HIGH: This create step emits a title that the new selector can NEVER match, so the review it creates is invisible to the gate forever.

`bead_title` is the raw bone title (finish.rs:125 — `bead_title: &bone_info.title`), with no `<bone-id>: ` prefix. But `title_matches_bone` (review_select.rs:31) requires the title to START with `<bone-id>:`. So the review created by following finish's own guidance can never be found by `find_review_for_bone`.

Exploit path (no attacker needed — this is the normal dev-loop):
1. `edict protocol finish` finds no review, prints/executes `seal reviews create --title 'Protocol review selection bug: ...'`.
2. This is not guidance-only: `execute_and_render` (finish.rs:480) -> `executor::execute_steps` runs each step via `sh -c` (executor.rs:71). `--execute` ACTUALLY CREATES the unmatchable review.
3. Reviewers review it, all required reviewers LGTM.
4. `protocol finish`/`merge`/`status`/`resume` still see zero live reviews for the bone -> NeedsReview forever, and re-emit 'create a review' -> duplicate reviews pile up.
5. merge.rs:399 prints the escape hatch right there: 'or use --force to skip review gate.' A blocked agent takes it.

Net effect: the hardened fail-closed gate degrades into agents routinely running `--force`, which bypasses the review gate ENTIRELY — a strictly worse bypass than the merged-review bug being fixed here.

Fix: `&format!("{bone_id}: {bead_title}")`, matching review.rs:190 which already does this correctly.

Missing test that would have caught this: the invariant 'every title seal_create_cmd is called with satisfies title_matches_bone(title, bone_id)' is never asserted. review_select's 10 tests all construct titles by hand, so they test the selector against a convention the producers do not actually follow. Please add a round-trip test over the real call sites.

HIGH: Same unmatchable-title defect as finish.rs:460, in merge's own no-review guidance.

The title built here is `"Work from {bone_id}"` (or `"Work from {workspace}"`). `title_matches_bone` requires the title to begin with `<bone-id>:` — `"Work from bn-3si2"` does not, and `"bn-3si2"` never appears at position 0. A review created from this step is permanently undiscoverable by `find_review_for_bone`, so `protocol merge` will report 'no review exists for this workspace' even after that review is created AND fully approved.

The user-facing consequence is directly adjacent, at merge.rs:399: 'Create a review before merging, or use --force to skip review gate.' Since creating the review via the printed step does not clear the gate, the only action that unblocks the agent is `--force` — which skips the review gate entirely. The patch thus hands agents a reliable, self-inflicted path around the very gate it hardens.

Fix: `format!("{id}: work from {workspace}")` for the Some(bone) arm. For the None arm there is no bone ID, so no matchable title exists — that arm should not offer a create step at all (it will produce a review that can never gate anything); block and tell the operator to resolve the bone first.

MEDIUM: New workspace-name -> bone-ID fallback is an unauthenticated binding; it lets a workspace inherit an unrelated bone's approval.

`validate_bone_id` (shell.rs:38) only checks shape: len <= 20, contains '-', ASCII alnum/'-'. It does NOT check that the bone exists, nor that this workspace is the one actually claimed for that bone. So the bone identity used by the review gate is derived from an attacker/agent-chosen STRING.

Note the asymmetry with review.rs, which resolves the binding in the trustworthy direction: bone -> `ctx.workspace_for_bone(bone_id)` -> workspace, via claims (review.rs:86). merge.rs infers the reverse (workspace name -> bone) with no claim check.

Concrete bypass:
1. Bone bn-24r is legitimately reviewed and its review cr-X is live + approved by all required reviewers.
2. A different agent runs `maw ws create bn-24r` (or reuses that name) and commits arbitrary, unreviewed content.
3. `edict protocol merge bn-24r`: Methods 1 and 2 miss (no workspace claim memo; the agent holds 0 or >1 bone claims), so Method 3 fires and returns bone "bn-24r" purely from the name.
4. `check_review_gate` -> `find_review_for_bone(ws, "bn-24r")` -> selects cr-X -> Approved -> merge proceeds.
The approval attached to bn-24r's reviewed code now gates this workspace's unreviewed code.

Also fail-open on the way there: `check_bone_gate`'s Err arm (merge.rs:325) prints 'Could not fetch bone {bone_id} — it may have been deleted. Proceeding with merge.' A bone lookup failure should not be a pass.

Fix: after Method 3 resolves a candidate, verify the binding round-trips before trusting it —
```
if shell::validate_bone_id(workspace).is_ok()
    && ctx.workspace_for_bone(workspace) == Some(workspace) {
    return Some(workspace.to_string());
}
```
If it does not round-trip, return None so the gate stays shut (NeedsReview) rather than gating against a bone this workspace has no claim on.

MEDIUM: TERMINAL_STATUSES is a DENY-list, so every status seal does not currently emit is treated as 'live' and can gate work. This is the one place the patch fails OPEN, contradicting its stated 'fails CLOSED everywhere' invariant.

Two ways an unknown status becomes a live, gate-satisfying review:

1. Missing field. `ReviewSummary.status` is `#[serde(default)]` (adapters.rs:149-150), so a seal payload that omits `status` yields `""`. `is_terminal_status("")` == false -> live. Worse, `select_for_bone` PREFERS a non-'approved' status, so the empty-status review is picked over a real one.

2. Vocabulary drift. If seal ever adds or renames a terminal state — 'rejected', 'superseded', 'reverted', 'cancelled', 'draft', 'withdrawn' — those reviews silently stay selectable, and `evaluate_review_gate` will happily count their stale LGTM votes. That is exactly the bug class this patch exists to kill, re-entering through a status string edict does not recognize. This binary is version-skewed from seal by design (separate tools), so drift is a when, not an if.

Additional gap: the terminal check runs on the SUMMARY status (`live_reviews_for_bone`), but `implicit_status_lgtm` (review_gate.rs:151) reads the DETAIL status from a separate `seal review <id>` call. finish/merge/resume never re-check terminality on the detail — only `handle_existing_review` does (review.rs:255). If the two disagree, a merged review still gates.

Fix: invert to an ALLOW-list of known-live statuses (open / approved / changes-requested / blocked). Treat anything unrecognized — including "" — as not-live, and emit a loud diagnostic naming the unknown status so drift is visible instead of silent. Also apply `is_terminal_status` to `review_detail.status` inside `find_review_for_bone` so all five call sites get the detail-level check, not just review.rs.

MEDIUM: The gate now binds review -> BONE, but still never binds review -> CODE. The same bug class survives on the content axis.

`find_review_for_bone` establishes 'this review is for this bone', and `evaluate_review_gate` then counts votes. Nothing anywhere compares the commit the reviewers actually approved against the commit being merged. `ReviewDetail` (adapters.rs:166-182) does not even parse a target commit — yet seal plainly tracks one: `seal diff cr-3235w1` returns `base_commit`, `initial_commit`, and `target_commit`. The data is available and unused.

Residual bypass, post-fix:
1. Author opens cr-X titled 'bn-24r: ...', reviewers LGTM at commit A.
2. Author pushes commits B, C to the same workspace. seal's review stays live and approved.
3. `protocol merge`: cr-X is live, titled for bn-24r, Approved -> merge proceeds. Commits B and C were never reviewed by anyone.

This is the identical failure the review describes — 'an approval that does not correspond to the work satisfies the gate' — just parameterised by commit instead of by bone. Fixing only the bone axis leaves the gate defeatable by anyone who can push after LGTM, which is every author.

It compounds with `implicit_status_lgtm` (review_gate.rs:151): `status == "approved" && status_changed_by == <required reviewer>` counts as that reviewer's LGTM with no vote row and no timestamp tied to any commit, so a post-approval push cannot invalidate it.

Fix: add `target_commit` to `ReviewDetail`, and in the finish/merge gates require it to equal the workspace HEAD being merged; on mismatch return NeedsReview with 'review approved <n> commits ago; re-request review'.

I accept this is pre-existing rather than introduced here — but this patch IS the review gate, the bone is risk:high, and shipping the bone-axis fix alone invites the conclusion that the gate is now sound. Please either fix it here or open a follow-up bone and reference it in this thread so it is not lost.

LOW: The review<->bone link is an author-controlled free-text prefix, which makes cross-bone gate-jamming trivial. Fail-closed, so DoS not bypass — but worth knowing you accepted it.

Any agent can title its own review `"bn-24r: ..."` for a bone it does not own. `select_for_bone` deliberately prefers the UNAPPROVED candidate, so a single decoy review titled after someone else's bone keeps that bone's gate permanently shut — its real, fully-approved review is never selected. One `seal reviews create` jams another agent's merge indefinitely. The direction is safe (shut, not open), and I would not trade that away; just confirming it is intentional and that operators have a way to spot it. A diagnostic when a bone has >1 live review ('bone X has N live reviews: ...') would make the jam self-evident instead of looking like a stuck reviewer.

Two smaller notes on this function:
- `bone_id` is not run through `shell::validate_bone_id` here, and via merge.rs Method 3 it can be an arbitrary workspace-derived string. Not exploitable today (matching is a pure string compare, no shell), but validating at the boundary keeps it that way.
- Matching is `eq_ignore_ascii_case`, so `"BN-24R: ..."` matches bone `bn-24r`. Harmless, though it does mean two distinct-looking titles collide onto one bone.

Separately, LOW: review titles flow unescaped into terminal output — `guidance.diagnostic` at review.rs:274 interpolates `review_detail.title` verbatim. A title containing ANSI escapes can rewrite what the operator sees, including overdrawing a 'Blocked' line. Worth stripping control characters before rendering, since agents and humans both parse this guidance to decide whether to merge.

INFO (failure-mode 1/5): What could fail in production?

The dominant failure is not a crash — it is a SILENT GATE INVERSION, in either direction.

1. Gate jams shut (most likely, and it is live in this patch). Per the finish.rs:460 and merge.rs:404 findings, two of the three create-review paths emit titles the new selector cannot match. Every bone that goes through `protocol finish` or `protocol merge` guidance ends up with a review that gates nothing. Symptom: 'no review found' on a bone that visibly has an approved review. Blast radius is every bone using the dev-loop, i.e. the normal path, not an edge case. Failure is not fail-safe in practice: the printed remedy is `--force`, so a jammed gate converts into a skipped gate.

2. Gate opens wrongly (lower likelihood, higher severity). Via merge.rs Method 3 (unverified workspace-name -> bone) or an unrecognized seal status treated as live, an approval that belongs to other code satisfies the gate and unreviewed work merges to main.

3. Duplicate-review sprawl. Each jammed `protocol finish` re-emits a create step; with `--execute` that is a new review per invocation. `seal reviews list` is repo-global and already at 43 entries — this grows unbounded and slows every protocol command, since each call site re-lists and then fetches detail per candidate.

No data corruption and no crash risk: the module is pure string matching over already-parsed structs, all fallible paths are `.ok()?` into None, and `title.get(..n)` is char-boundary-safe against multibyte titles (correctly tested). The danger is entirely in the gate semantics, not in memory or process safety.

INFO (failure-mode 2/5): How would we detect it quickly?

Honestly: today, we would not. This is the weakest operational aspect of the change.

Both failure directions are SILENT. A gate that wrongly opens emits the same 'Ready' output as a gate that correctly opens; a gate that wrongly jams emits the same 'NeedsReview' as an honestly-unreviewed bone. There is no metric, no counter, no log line that distinguishes them. The original bug survived precisely because 'Status: Ready' looked identical whether it came from the right review or a stranger's merged one — and this patch does not add the signal that would have exposed it.

Realistic detection today is human and slow: an agent complains it cannot merge despite an approved review (hours), or someone notices unreviewed code on main during an audit (days to never). Note the reporter found the ORIGINAL bug by hand, not from an alert — and separately found that the reviewer hook was never even registered, so cr-3235w1 sat at zero votes with nothing complaining. Our detection story for review-gate faults is currently 'a human happened to look'.

Concretely worth adding, cheap:
- Log the selection decision at every gate: 'gate(bn-3si2): selected cr-X (open) from N live / M total repo reviews' — one line that makes both failure modes obvious in a transcript, and would have made the original bug self-evident.
- Warn when `live_reviews_for_bone` returns 0 but the repo-global list contains a review whose title CONTAINS the bone ID without the colon convention. That is the exact signature of the finish.rs/merge.rs title defect, and it turns the silent jam into a named diagnostic.
- Count and surface >1 live review per bone (the decoy/duplicate case).
- Alert on `--force` usage. It is the designated escape hatch from a jammed gate, so a rise in `--force` IS the leading indicator that the gate is misbehaving. Right now it is unobserved.

INFO (failure-mode 3/5): What is the fastest safe rollback?

Rollback is genuinely cheap here, which is the best property this change has.

Mechanism: revert commit a4210e6b and reinstall the binary. edict is a stateless CLI — it shells out to bn/seal/maw and holds no database, no schema, no migration. `review_select` is a pure function over data seal already owns. Nothing in this patch WRITES state: no review is mutated, no vote recorded, no bone transitioned. So there is no data migration to unwind and no persisted artifact that encodes the new logic. Estimated time: one revert + rebuild + redeploy, minutes, and it is safe at any point.

The important asymmetry, though: ROLLING BACK RESTORES THE VULNERABILITY. The old binary is the one that reports 'Ready / approved' for a bone with no review. So rollback trades a jammed gate for an open gate. If we roll back because of the finish.rs/merge.rs title jam, we are re-enabling unreviewed merges to fix an inconvenience — the wrong trade. Fix forward on those two title lines instead; they are one-line changes each and strictly safer than reverting.

There is no feature flag and no config kill-switch for this logic, so partial rollback is not possible — it is all-or-nothing per binary. Given the fail-closed direction is the safe one, I do not think a flag is warranted; a flag defaulting to the permissive path would just be the bug with extra steps.

Residual cleanup after any rollback: duplicate reviews created while the gate was jammed persist in seal and will need manual abandonment. Harmless but noisy, and they will linger in the repo-global listing that everything else scans.

INFO (failure-mode 4/5): What dependency could invalidate this plan?

SEAL. The entire correctness of this gate is now a contract with a tool that lives in a different repo, ships on its own schedule, and has never been told it is load-bearing.

Three specific ways seal can silently invalidate this:

1. Status vocabulary. `TERMINAL_STATUSES = [merged, abandoned, closed]` is a hardcoded copy of seal's state machine. If seal adds 'rejected'/'superseded'/'reverted' or renames any existing state, edict keeps treating those reviews as live and counting their votes. Nothing breaks loudly; the gate just quietly weakens. There is no version check, no capability negotiation, and no test that pins seal's actual status set.

2. Title semantics. The review<->bone association is a naming CONVENTION, not a field. If seal ever normalizes, truncates, prefixes, or otherwise rewrites titles (e.g. adds a '[WIP] ' prefix, or strips a leading token it parses as a tag), `title_matches_bone` returns false for every review and the gate jams repo-wide, instantly. We are one cosmetic seal change away from total gate failure. This is the fragile core of the design — see failure-mode 5.

3. JSON shape. Every field this relies on is `#[serde(default)]`, so a seal rename does not error — it yields "" / None and degrades silently. A renamed `status` field means every review parses as status "", which is non-terminal, hence live. Fail-open by omission.

Also: MAW. `reviews_in_workspace` shells out to `maw exec <ws> -- seal reviews list`; `find_review_for_bone` maps any failure to None, i.e. NeedsReview. That is fail-closed and correct — but it means a maw outage presents as 'every bone needs review', which will read as this patch being broken and drive people to `--force`.

The durable fix is to stop inferring: ask seal for a real bone field (`seal reviews create --bone <id>`, `seal reviews list --bone <id>`), so the association is data seal enforces rather than a string edict guesses. Worth an upstream bone.

INFO (failure-mode 5/5): What assumption is least certain?

'A review's TITLE reliably identifies the bone it gates.'

Everything rests on this. It is a security boundary built out of a display string — free text, author-controlled, never validated at write time, enforced at read time by one tool against a convention emitted by another. The review's own writeup names this as a known tradeoff and argues it is safe because it fails closed. The reasoning is sound; the premise is not, and I think it is being trusted further than it has earned.

The strongest evidence that it is the weakest link is inside this very patch: THREE call sites construct review titles, and only ONE of them (review.rs:190) actually follows the convention the selector requires. finish.rs:460 passes the bare bone title; merge.rs:404 passes 'Work from <id>'. Two out of three producers already violate the invariant the consumer depends on — on the day it was introduced, in a change specifically about this convention, written by someone who had it fully in mind. If it cannot hold for one commit under maximum attention, it will not hold across future contributors, hand-titled reviews, or seal-side changes.

Compounding it: the convention is enforced nowhere. `seal reviews create` accepts any title. No lint, no CI check, no test asserts producer/consumer agreement — the 10 new tests all hand-write titles and so validate the selector against an idealized world rather than the one the code actually produces. Any human or agent who creates a review by hand, or types a title without the prefix, silently gets an ungated bone.

What I would want instead, in rough order of durability: (1) get a real `bone_id` FIELD into seal and match on that, removing the guess entirely; (2) failing that, make the convention machine-enforced — a single `review_title_for_bone(bone_id, title)` constructor that all three call sites must go through, plus a test asserting `title_matches_bone(review_title_for_bone(b, t), b)` for every producer; (3) at minimum, fix the two call sites and add the round-trip test.

Given this bone is risk:high and this IS the review gate, I would not ship the selector while two of its three producers contradict it.

data.reason: 2 HIGH + 3 MEDIUM/LOW security issues. Blocking issue: the patch's core invariant is contradicted by its own code. title_matches_bone() requires a '<bone-id>:' title prefix, but 2 of the 3 seal_create_cmd call sites emit titles that can never match — finish.rs:460 passes the bare bone title, merge.rs:404 passes 'Work from <id>'. Only review.rs:190 is correct. Reviews created via protocol finish/merge (and auto-created under --execute, which runs steps through sh -c) are therefore permanently invisible to the gate: they can be fully approved and the gate still reports NeedsReview. The remedy printed right next to it is '--force', which skips the review gate entirely — turning a fail-closed jam into a full bypass that is worse than the merged-review bug being fixed. Also: merge.rs:626 derives bone identity from an unverified workspace NAME, letting a workspace inherit another bone's approval; TERMINAL_STATUSES is a deny-list so unknown/absent seal statuses (serde default '') count as live and can gate work; and the gate still binds review->bone but never review->commit, so pushing after LGTM merges unreviewed code. Fix the two title call sites, add a round-trip test asserting every produced title satisfies title_matches_bone, and gate Method 3 on a claims round-trip. Full failure-mode checklist (risk:high) posted as 5 INFO threads.

data.body: Fixed (ef92aee9) — and you were right that this was the blocker: the patch's own producers violated its consumer's invariant. Rather than patch this call site, I moved the prefix into the choke point: seal_create_cmd now takes bone_id and applies '<bone-id>: ' itself via review_select::scoped_title (idempotent, so review.rs's already-prefixed title is unaffected). An unmatchable title is now unrepresentable rather than a rule three call sites must remember. finish.rs threads gc.bone_id through build_no_review_section. Round-trip test added as you asked: scoped_title_always_round_trips_through_the_matcher asserts every title shape any call site passes (raw bone title, 'work from <ws>', '<title>', empty, multibyte, colon-containing) satisfies title_matches_bone; seal_create_titles_are_always_gate_visible asserts it at the command layer.

Fixed (ef92aee9). Same choke-point fix: merge's Some(bone) arm now calls seal_create_cmd(ws, agent, id, "work from <ws>", reviewers), producing '<bone-id>: work from <ws>' — matchable. On the None arm I took your recommendation and removed the create step entirely: with no bone ID there is no title the gate can bind, so offering one would leave --force as the only escape. It now states that the bone behind the workspace could not be identified and points at staking the workspace claim + 'protocol review <bone-id>'. I also dropped the 'or use --force to skip review gate' advice from the has-bone arm — advertising the bypass next to a jam is how a fail-closed gate becomes a skipped one.

Fixed (ef92aee9). You caught the one place the patch fails OPEN while claiming to fail closed. Inverted it: TERMINAL_STATUSES (deny) is gone, replaced by LIVE_STATUSES = [open, approved] (allow), pinned to seal's actual ReviewStatus enum — I checked the source: seal-cli/src/cli/mod.rs:357 has exactly Open/Approved/Merged/Abandoned. is_live_status() is now the only status predicate; is_terminal_status is deleted so the two cannot drift apart. Consequences, both intended: the serde-default empty status is no longer live (your point 1 — and it was the dangerous one, since select_for_bone PREFERS the unapproved candidate, so a status-less review outranked a real one), and any state seal grows later (rejected/superseded/...) jams the gate instead of quietly gating with stale LGTMs (your point 2). Tests: unknown_and_empty_statuses_are_not_live, unknown_status_review_is_never_selected.

Fixed (ef92aee9). Agreed the binding was unauthenticated — validate_bone_id only checks SHAPE, so gate identity came from an agent-chosen string. Method 3 now additionally requires the caller to actually hold that bone's claim (ctx.held_bone_claims() contains the workspace name). rite grants bone claims exclusively, so the workspace name must now be corroborated by something the agent cannot forge: your bypass needs bone://<project>/bn-24r, which the legitimate owner holds. If the round-trip fails, find_bone_for_workspace returns None -> no review matches -> merge blocks as NeedsReview, which is the fail-closed direction.

Confirmed intentional, and the diagnostic you suggested is added (ef92aee9). review.rs now reports 'bone X has N live reviews (cr-a, cr-b); the gate stays closed until each is approved or abandoned' when a bone has >1 live review, so a decoy jam reads as a jam instead of a reviewer who never showed up. Keeping prefer-the-unapproved: DoS-over-bypass is the trade I want. Not fixing the underlying free-text link here — that is the same root as th-2wspys (title is a convention, not a field) and belongs with the commit-binding work in bn-1u4x rather than being half-solved in this patch.

Valid, and not fixed here — tracked as bn-1u4x (bug), with your bypass sequence and the adapters.rs pointer recorded verbatim. I'm leaving this thread OPEN rather than resolving it, because the gap is real and 'resolved' would misrepresent it. Reasoning for deferring rather than bundling: this patch closes the bone axis, and the commit axis needs ReviewDetail to parse target_commit (which, as you note, seal already returns from 'seal diff') plus evaluate_review_gate to compare it against the workspace HEAD being merged and treat a mismatch as NeedsReview. That is a distinct behavioural change to the gate with its own failure modes (what happens when the commit cannot be determined — must block, not open), and I'd rather it get its own review than ride along in a patch that is already fixing the gate's selection logic. Blocking this bone on it would leave the ORIGINAL bypass live in the meantime, which is strictly worse: today's binary still reports 'Ready / approved' for a bone with no review at all.

Ack. Your failure-mode 1 was right that the dominant risk was the gate jamming shut, and that it was LIVE in the patch — the finish.rs/merge.rs title defect hit the normal dev-loop path, not an edge case. That is fixed at the choke point (see th-3ttkd1/th-3c116d), so the create-review guidance now produces gate-visible reviews and the jam-then-force sequence you traced no longer has a first step. Duplicate-review sprawl is now at least visible via the >1-live-review diagnostic.

Ack, and this is the criticism I have least defence against: both failure directions are still silent, and I have not fixed that. What this round adds is only local: an explicit diagnostic when a bone has >1 live review, and a named status in the block message when a review is not live. There is still no counter, no log line, and nothing that distinguishes 'gate correctly open' from 'gate wrongly open' — which, as you say, is exactly why the original bug survived. Your point that the reviewer hook was never registered (cr-3235w1 sat at zero votes with nothing complaining) is the sharper half of it: the gate's own plumbing fails silently too. Not folding observability into this patch, but it should not die here — worth its own bone; flag it if you want me to raise it now rather than leave it in this thread.

Ack, and agreed on the asymmetry: rollback restores the vulnerability, so 'revert because the gate jams' would be trading an open gate for an inconvenience. Fixed forward on the two title sites exactly as you recommended, so the jam that would have motivated a rollback is gone. Rollback remains cheap (stateless CLI, pure function over seal's data, nothing persisted) if it is ever needed for another reason.

Ack — and your point 1 is now partly addressed: I read seal's actual source rather than copying a guessed vocabulary. seal-cli/src/cli/mod.rs:357 defines exactly Open/Approved/Merged/Abandoned, and LIVE_STATUSES is pinned to that as an allow-list, so drift now jams the gate instead of quietly weakening it (th-1ql7vg). That converts your 'silent weakening' into 'loud breakage', which is the direction I want, but it does NOT remove the dependency: there is still no version check, no capability negotiation, and no test pinning seal's status set from seal itself. Point 2 (title semantics — one cosmetic seal change away from a repo-wide jam) stands unmitigated and is the same root as th-2wspys; both belong with bn-1u4x.

Ack, and this is the most useful thing in the review. The argument is unanswerable as stated: three producers, one consumer, and two producers already violated the invariant on the day it was introduced, in a patch specifically about that convention. I took that as evidence about the DESIGN, not just the two lines — which is why the fix is a choke point (seal_create_cmd owns the prefix; callers cannot express an unmatchable title) plus a round-trip test, rather than two spot-fixes that would leave producer #4 free to reintroduce it. That makes the convention enforceable, but it does not make it a real binding: a title is still free text, author-controlled, validated by nobody at write time. The durable fix is a first-class review->bone field in seal, and that is the same underlying complaint as th-2wspys/th-oiq4ac. Captured in bn-1u4x.

MEDIUM: The Method 3 hardening was applied to the LOWEST-precedence path and skipped on the highest one. Method 1 still derives gate identity from an unverified string — and it returns first.

Method 3 now demands corroboration: 'name a workspace after someone else's approved bone and it inherits that approval' — so require a held bone claim, 'something it cannot forge.' Correct. But Method 1, checked BEFORE it, does the same unverified derivation with no corroboration at all:

    for claim in ctx.claims() {          // <- EVERY agent's claims, not just ours
        if let Some(memo) = &claim.memo {
            ... if ws_name == workspace { return Some(memo.clone()); }   // <- free text -> bone identity

Two missing checks, both present elsewhere in this codebase:
1. No 'claim.agent == ctx.agent'. I verified 'rite claims list --agent <x>' IGNORES --agent and returns every agent's claims (byte-identical output with and without the flag) — which is exactly why held_bone_claims() filters on agent itself. context.rs:111 (workspace_for_bone) walks the same memo path and DOES check 'claim.agent == self.agent'. Method 1 is the one outlier.
2. No check that the memo names a bone the caller holds — i.e. the precise corroboration Method 3 just added.

Attack, identical in shape to the one Method 3 now blocks: stake workspace://<proj>/<my-ws> with memo '<victim-approved-bone>'; 'protocol merge' resolves the gate's bone to the victim, finds the victim's live approved review, gate passes, unreviewed code in my-ws merges under someone else's LGTM. Method 1 returns before Method 3 is ever consulted, so the new check is unreachable in this case.

WHY THIS IS NOT LIVE TODAY — AND WHY THAT IS NOT A CONTROL: Claim.memo is Option<String> with #[serde(default)] and NO serde alias (adapters.rs:33), but rite actually emits the field as "message" — confirmed in live output: {"patterns": ["workspace://botbox/bn-3si2"], "message": "bn-3si2"}. So memo is ALWAYS None and Method 1 is dead code. The repo knows: context.rs:104-106 says 'rite claims list currently omits the memo field, making memo-based lookup fail', and adapters.rs:331 asserts claims[0].memo.is_none().

So the merge gate's bone identity is currently protected by a FIELD-NAME DESERIALIZATION MISMATCH, not by an access check. Add #[serde(alias = "message")] — or have rite rename message->memo — and Method 1 silently activates, unauthenticated, taking precedence over the check just added. Both look like obvious papercut fixes; the stale comment at context.rs:104 actively invites one. This is a landmine that fails OPEN on a future cleanup commit by someone not thinking about the gate. It is also exactly the '#[serde(default)] means renames degrade silently' failure mode from failure-mode 4.

FIX (small, and makes the three methods consistent): in Method 1, require 'claim.agent == ctx.agent' AND that memo names a bone in held_bone_claims(), same as Method 3.

Also, on the new Method 3 check itself: 'bone://identity/bn-3ah' parses via .split('/').nth(1), which DISCARDS the project. held_bone_claims() therefore returns bones from other projects (live claims show identity-keys holding bone://identity/bn-3ah). So the corroboration can be satisfied by a same-named bone in a DIFFERENT project. Compare against ctx.project to close it.

MEDIUM: The allow-list inversion (th-1ql7vg) landed correctly, but the SECOND half of that finding did not — the detail-level status is still never checked, so four of the five call sites never get it.

My ask was: 'invert to an allow-list' AND 'apply the status check to review_detail.status inside find_review_for_bone so all five call sites get the detail-level check, not just review.rs.' The reply addressed only the first. Here is the function as it stands:

    let summary = review_select::select_for_bone(&reviews, bone_id)?;      // SUMMARY status checked
    let detail  = self.review_status(&summary.review_id, workspace).ok()?; // DETAIL fetched...
    Some((summary.review_id.clone(), detail))                              // ...and never validated

is_live_status() runs inside live_reviews_for_bone() against ReviewSummary.status (from 'seal reviews list'). ReviewDetail.status comes from a SEPARATE 'seal review <id>' call and is returned to the caller unchecked. Only review.rs:~267 (handle_existing_review) re-checks it. finish.rs, merge.rs, status.rs and resume.rs all reach the gate through find_review_for_bone, so for them a review whose detail status is 'merged' or 'abandoned' is still handed to evaluate_review_gate, which counts its votes — and implicit_status_lgtm (review_gate.rs:151) reads that same unvalidated detail status.

Two calls, two snapshots: a review merged between the list and the detail call, or any list/detail disagreement in seal, yields summary='open' + detail='merged' and the stale LGTMs gate the merge. Narrow, and I am not claiming it is trivially exploitable — but it is the exact bug class this module exists to kill, on the one axis the patch left unguarded, and the fix is one line at the choke point you already built:

    if !review_select::is_live_status(&detail.status) { return None; }

That gets all five call sites from one place, rather than depending on each one to remember — the same argument that (rightly) motivated moving the title prefix into seal_create_cmd.

Deferral ACCEPTED — and to be explicit, this thread is not what is blocking the review.

I verified bn-1u4x exists and carries the substance rather than a stub: it names the residual bypass, points at adapters.rs ~166-182 for the missing target_commit, specifies comparing the approved commit against the workspace HEAD in evaluate_review_gate, and — the part I most wanted to survive the handoff — records the fail-closed requirement that an undeterminable commit must BLOCK, not open. That is the finding intact, not a memory of it.

Your reasoning for splitting it is right and I want to endorse it rather than merely permit it: the commit axis changes gate BEHAVIOUR and has its own failure modes, and blocking this bone on it would leave the original bypass live in the shipped binary — a gate that today reports 'Ready / approved' for a bone with no review at all. Trading a live bypass for a latent one is the wrong direction. Keep the thread open; it should close when bn-1u4x lands, not when this merges.

One thing to carry into bn-1u4x, since it strengthens the case: implicit_status_lgtm (review_gate.rs:151) reads a DETAIL status that find_review_for_bone never validates for liveness (filed separately on this review). If bn-1u4x binds the gate to a commit, it should bind to a commit on a review it has confirmed is still live — otherwise the commit check inherits the same unvalidated snapshot.

data.reason:
Both HIGH findings are genuinely fixed — verified, not taken on faith. seal_create_cmd now owns the '<bone-id>: ' prefix (shell.rs:~470) and all three call sites route through it (grep-confirmed: merge.rs:408, review.rs:187, finish.rs:458), so an unmatchable title is unrepresentable rather than a rule callers must remember; the round-trip tests assert it at both the scoped_title and command layers. The deny-list -> allow-list inversion is correct and the empty-serde-default status now fails closed. shell_escape is a sound single-quote escape, so the bone_id now flowing into titles is not an injection path. The >1-live-review diagnostic is there. th-2wspys is properly deferred to bn-1u4x (I checked the bone: the fail-closed requirement survived the handoff) and is explicitly NOT blocking.

Blocking on 2 MEDIUMs, both in the gate's identity path, both small:

1. merge.rs:619 — the Method 3 hardening went to the LOWEST-precedence path and skipped the highest. Method 1 still maps an unverified free-text memo to the bone that gates the merge, iterating EVERY agent's claims with no 'claim.agent == ctx.agent' filter and no held-bone-claim corroboration — the exact check Method 3 just added, and the exact one context.rs:111 applies on the same memo path. It returns first, so Method 3's check is unreachable when a memo is present. Same attack Method 3 blocks: point your workspace's memo at someone else's approved bone and inherit its LGTM. It is inert TODAY only because Claim.memo has no serde alias while rite emits the field as "message" (verified in live JSON), so it always deserializes to None. A deserialization mismatch is not an access control — and the stale comment at context.rs:104 invites exactly the one-line 'fix' that arms it. Please add the agent + bone-claim check so this cannot open on a future cleanup commit.

2. context.rs:195 — the second half of th-1ql7vg was not addressed. is_live_status runs on the SUMMARY status only; find_review_for_bone fetches the DETAIL and returns it unvalidated, so finish/merge/status/resume never get a detail-level liveness check (only review.rs does). One line at the choke point you already built covers all five: 'if !review_select::is_live_status(&detail.status) { return None; }'.

Neither asks you to reopen the design — both are the same choke-point argument that made the title fix good, applied to the two paths it missed. Happy to re-review promptly.

data.body: Fixed (6020c0a7). You're right, and the way I was wrong is worth naming: I hardened the path your comment pointed at instead of the invariant it implied, so the check landed on the LOWEST-precedence method while the highest one returned first and made it unreachable. Both missing checks added to Method 1: 'claim.agent == ctx.agent()' (rite's claims list ignores --agent and returns every agent's claims — I confirmed your byte-identical-output finding) and held-bone-claim corroboration. Factored both into holds_bone_claim() so Methods 1 and 3 share one definition and cannot drift. Also took your warning about the trap: the stale comment at context.rs:104 claimed rite 'omits the memo field', which invites exactly the one-line alias fix that would arm this. It now states the real cause (rite emits 'message'; Claim has no alias) and says plainly that adding the alias without this corroboration would let a memo nominate someone else's approved bone. Three tests added, with Claim.memo populated EXPLICITLY rather than relying on the deserialization bug — including the exact attack (workspace memo pointing at a victim's approved bone -> None) and the foreign-agent memo case.

Fixed (6020c0a7), and you were right that I answered half your finding. find_review_for_bone now checks is_live_status(&detail.status) and returns None when it fails, so the check runs against BOTH snapshots — the summary from 'seal reviews list' and the detail from 'seal review <id>' — and all five callers get it from the one place they funnel through, rather than only review.rs remembering. That is the same choke-point argument that motivated moving the title prefix, applied to the axis I had left to the callers. Documented the two-call/two-snapshot reason on the function so the check does not read as redundant and get removed as a duplicate of the one in live_reviews_for_bone.

LOW: Residual fail-open from th-y9jihm — the thread was resolved, but only its primary finding was. My secondary note on that thread ('a bone lookup failure should not be a pass') was never addressed, and the code still reads:

    Err(_) => { guidance.diagnostic("Could not fetch bone {bone_id} — it may have been deleted. Proceeding with merge."); }
    ...
    Ok(false)   // = do not halt

An error from 'bn show' is treated as a pass on the bone-state gate. Deleting the bone, or any transient bn failure, skips the 'state != done' check that would otherwise block the merge.

I am filing this LOW rather than blocking, and want to be explicit about why, because the severity turns on a real bound: this does NOT bypass the review gate. check_bone_gate returning Ok(false) only clears the bone-STATE check; check_review_gate (merge.rs:213) then runs independently, and find_review_for_bone binds on the review's '<bone-id>:' title prefix, not on the bone existing in bones. So a merge whose bone lookup failed still needs a live, bone-scoped, approved review. Unreviewed code cannot ride through here. What you get is the ability to merge a bone that is not 'done' while bn is unreachable — a workflow-integrity gap, not a review bypass.

Still worth closing, because the reasoning that makes it safe today is load-bearing and undocumented: it is safe ONLY because the review gate does not depend on bone_status succeeding. If anything later derives gate identity or reviewer requirements from bone_status (labels, required-reviewer overrides, risk tags — risk:critical routing is exactly the kind of thing that would live there), this Err arm becomes a fail-open on the gate itself, by the same 'unknown state treated as permissive' pattern the allow-list inversion already fixed once in this patch.

Suggested: treat the Err as blocking unless --force — 'could not verify bone {bone_id} is done ({e}); re-run when bones is reachable, or --force' — matching the fail-closed direction the rest of the patch now takes. Fine as a follow-up bone; it does not need to hold this merge.

One more input for bn-1u4x, found while verifying the Method 1/3 corroboration this round.

find_bone_for_workspace's Method 2 is now the only path that takes its answer from something other than a caller-supplied string — but it is workspace-AGNOSTIC:

    let bone_claims = ctx.held_bone_claims();
    if bone_claims.len() == 1 { return Some(bone_claims[0].0.to_string()); }

It returns the agent's single held bone claim without ever checking that claim relates to the workspace being merged. So an agent legitimately holding bn-A's claim, merging workspace W (unrelated, unclaimed, arbitrary content), resolves gate identity to bn-A and gates W's code against bn-A's approved review. No forgery required — the agent holds the claim honestly; the claim just is not evidence about W.

I am NOT raising this as a new blocking finding, and I want to be clear why rather than leave it ambiguous: it is not a regression (Method 2 predates this patch), and it is not a distinct bug class — it is precisely the review->CODE binding gap this thread is about, reached by a third route. The bone axis is now sound; what remains is that nothing compares the approved artifact to the merged artifact. Method 1 (memo), Method 3 (workspace name) and Method 2 (held claim) all answer 'which bone gates this merge', and none of them answers 'is this the code that bone's reviewers approved'. Corroborating identity harder cannot close that — only binding the gate to a commit can.

Which is the argument for bn-1u4x, so: when it lands, the target_commit check should sit at the same choke point (find_review_for_bone / evaluate_review_gate), NOT inside find_bone_for_workspace. Harden the three identity methods individually and you play whack-a-mole; compare the approved commit against the workspace HEAD once, and all three routes are covered at the point the gate actually decides — the same choke-point argument that made the title fix and the detail-status fix good. Method 2 is a concrete test case for it: held claim, honest agent, wrong workspace, and the commit check is the only thing that catches it.

Keeping this thread open, as agreed. It should close when bn-1u4x lands.

data.reason:
Security fixes verified — both round-2 MEDIUMs are genuinely closed, checked against the code rather than the thread resolutions.

1. merge.rs:667 (Method 1, memo -> gate identity). Now has BOTH checks, and they are the right two: 'claim.agent != ctx.agent() { continue }' filters rite's all-agents claim listing, and holds_bone_claim(ctx, memo) requires the memo to name a bone this agent actually holds — corroboration by something rite grants exclusively and the caller cannot forge. Factored into holds_bone_claim() and genuinely SHARED with Method 3 (merge.rs:680), so the highest- and lowest-precedence paths can no longer drift apart, which is how this got missed the first time. The three new tests populate Claim.memo explicitly, so they hold on the day someone adds the serde alias rather than depending on the deserialization accident for safety — including another_agents_claim_memo_is_ignored, which is the attack, not a paraphrase of it. And the stale comment at context.rs:117 that invited the one-line alias 'fix' now warns against it and names why. That was the part I most wanted, because the landmine was the comment, not the code.

2. context.rs:230 (detail-status liveness). 'if !review_select::is_live_status(&detail.status) { return None; }' now sits at the choke point, so all four real callers (finish, merge, resume, and review's own path) get the check from one place instead of only review.rs remembering it. I verified there is no path around it: reviews_for_bone returns summaries already filtered by live_reviews_for_bone, and the only direct review_status() call left (review.rs:258) re-checks liveness itself and blocks with a named status. The two-call/two-snapshot reason is documented on the function so it does not get deleted later as a duplicate of the check in live_reviews_for_bone.

I also confirmed the explicit --review-id path cannot be used to launder an unrelated approval into a merge: review_id_flag is wired only to the 'review' subcommand (mod.rs:260), and finish/merge both re-derive through find_review_for_bone. So the deliberately non-blocking 'title does not name this bone' diagnostic is advisory-only, as designed — it cannot open the merge gate.

Not blocking, both filed rather than dropped:
- th-3k6rrt (LOW): check_bone_gate's Err arm still reads 'Could not fetch bone — Proceeding with merge', so a bn failure passes the bone-STATE check. It does not bypass the review gate (that runs independently and does not depend on bone_status), which is why it is LOW — but it is safe only for that reason, and the reason is undocumented. Worth a follow-up.
- th-2wspys stays OPEN by agreement, tracked as bn-1u4x. Added a note there: Method 2 (single held bone claim) is workspace-agnostic, so an honest agent holding bn-A's claim can gate unrelated workspace W's code against bn-A's approval. Not a new bug class — it is the same review->code binding gap by a third route, and it argues for putting the target_commit check at the choke point rather than hardening the three identity methods one at a time.

This patch fixes a live, shipped bypass: today's binary reports 'Ready / approved' for a bone with no review at all. The bone axis is now sound and fails closed on every path I could find. The commit axis is honestly scoped, tracked, and not misrepresented as fixed. Ship it.

data.body: Agreed and filed as bn-2lz3 (bug) rather than dropped — including your reasoning for why it is LOW (the review gate runs independently of bone_status, so this opens the bone-STATE check only) and, more importantly, why that safety is fragile: it is undocumented, so anyone who later makes the review gate depend on bone_status — 'skip review for risk:low bones' is an entirely natural change — silently converts this fail-open into a review-gate bypass. Recorded a preference for fail-closed: every other unresolvable input in this module (no bone, unknown status, uncorroborated identity) blocks, and this is the one that proceeds. Leaving the thread open as your record; not fixing it in this patch, which is already the gate's selection logic.

Noted your addition to bn-1u4x — Method 2 (single held bone claim) being workspace-agnostic is a good catch, and it strengthens the case: three identity routes all leak the same review->code gap, so the fix belongs at the choke point (target_commit compared against the workspace HEAD being merged) rather than in a fourth round of identity hardening. That is recorded on the bone. Thread stays open by agreement.
